If you wait to get organized, you're not in the money attraction business, you're in the getting organized business. I have a person in coaching, I finally got rid of him this year, but I had him for three years. And every conversation was all about what he was going to do, keywords going to. Not what I'm doing, going to. Another guy, he's my favorite. Two years, phone call every month to talk about the sales letter. No, no, no, the same sales letter. The sales letter that never got mailed. He was working on the sales letter, trying to develop the perfect sales letter. And after two years, he's still sending me, you know, 18 new drafts to look at this month before the phone call about the sales letter. Well, first of all, at this point, I don't care. Secondly, at this point, you couldn't, I can't even tell anymore what's good and what's bad. It's impossible. I mean, and third of all, we could have found out more two years ago by mailing some of them. I mean, by some stamps, you know, get the thing in the mail. Well, I'm waiting for this, I'm waiting for that. In the Renegade Millionaire System, I talk about the fact that the wealthy don't do anything sequentially. They do a whole bunch of things simultaneously. And so it's opposite of how we're all taught to behave, right? And so, so if you think about money in general, here's what everybody tries to do. They try and get wealthy sequentially, meaning they try and make some money. And then when they're making more money than they need to eat, house, etc., then they try and save some money. Then when they've saved up enough money to make it worse, scurn around with investing, then they finally get around to investing money. But the sequential thing, they never get there. You got to make save, invest all at the same time, because you got to take it off the top of every dollar that comes in here. So people do business the same way. They try and do everything sequentially. But that's not how you get big things done, and that's not how you attract money to you. Because money is attracted to something going on that is big and exciting and dramatic and dynamic and chaotic. It's not attracted to the tiny, timid, methodical, sequential things. It like doesn't notice any more than the rest of the world notices. If you look at Trump, I think one of the most interesting things about Trump, of all of the things that are interesting about Trump, is that nobody makes more affirming statements per minute than he does. Watch him now when he's interviewed. People make fun of it. They don't get what he's doing, so they make fun of it. But every statement's in affirming, every project's going to be the greatest, it is going to be the greatest project anybody's ever seen. It's going to be the greatest golf course ever built on a planet Earth. Oh, this is going to be the greatest season. This is going to be the greatest show. This is fabulous. This is terrific. This is, listen to him. And everything's big. Tallest building, biggest building, biggest golf course, biggest golf course built on a slant. Biggest this, grandest this, greatest that. Well, you get people really excited by saying, you know, we're building the 19th best thing, you know, or the 27th biggest thing. You don't draw much attention with that and you don't attract money with that. So you act, but you got to act in a big way to inspire yourself and others. Larger than life. Remember, that's what people want. So that's the process for putting all the forces in motion that move money to you. I would like to talk a little bit more about price with you. Price is sort of a representation of how people think about money. So what did you think this morning? I asked rhetorically, you can answer to yourself, but what did you think this morning? What ran through your mind when Caruthers said, $1100, we raised the price to $30,000?
